title: Truth-Telling, Owning Mistakes & Making Things Right

required.title: [For Mentor] Parenting with Love and Logic
required.author: Foster Cline and Jim Fay

required.title: [For Mentor] How to Talk So Kids Will Listen & Listen So Kids Will Talk
required.author: Adele Faber and Elaine Mazlish

required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of honest people and the cost of dishonesty

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] The Empty Pot
suggested.author: Demi

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Sam, Bangs & Moonshine
suggested.author: Evaline Ness

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] George Washington and the Cherry Tree (traditional)

supplementary.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Biographies of people known for integrity

competence_markers.description: They tell the truth about mistakes and misbehavior without needing to be caught first — voluntarily, as their first response
They own their actions directly, without deflecting blame to siblings, circumstances, or anyone else
They can deliver a specific, sincere apology that names the action, acknowledges the effect, and includes a commitment to change
They make restitution when their actions create a cost for others — cleaning up, fixing, replacing, or doing extra work to address what they caused
They do not lie to escape consequences — even when the truth means short-term discomfort
They are developing the ability to self-correct after a lie — going back to tell the truth voluntarily when they catch themselves being dishonest
Their pattern of honesty is improving over time — truth-telling is becoming more reflexive and less effortful
They engage honestly in the weekly honesty check-in, including naming times they were tempted to be dishonest

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it mean to tell the truth?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is it sometimes hard? Can you think of a time when telling the truth was hard but you did it anyway?
Not just the punishment — what happens to trust? What happens inside the person who lied? What happens between the liar and the person they lied to?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you made a mistake and owned it.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you do? What did you say? How did it feel? What happened after?
What are the parts? What makes it different from just saying "sorry"?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about someone from a story who was honest even when it was hard.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they do? What did it cost them? Was it worth it?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it mean to make something right?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you think of a time you made something right after you made a mistake? What did you do?

cross_connections.description: IC1.3 and IC1.1 run in parallel. The self-governance developed in IC1.1 provides the self-control needed to tell the truth when lying would be easier. The accountability practiced in IC1.3 reinforces the ownership of behavior that IC1.1 develops.

Owning mistakes (IC1.3) and recovering from failure (IC1.4) are deeply intertwined. The child who learns to own mistakes without shame-collapse is practicing both sub-domains simultaneously. IC1.4 provides the emotional architecture that makes IC1.3 sustainable.

Honesty appears as a core virtue in every tradition IC1.2 surveys. The stories used in IC1.3 overlap directly with the virtue-as-story approach of IC1.2. The child encounters honesty as something every serious civilization has valued.
The weekly honesty check-in is an early form of self-examination. The child who can reflect on "when was I tempted to lie?" is building the interior awareness that IC1.6 develops more fully.

Telling the truth under pressure requires emotional regulation. The child must tolerate the discomfort of potential consequences without being overwhelmed by it. IC3.1 builds the capacity; IC1.3 provides the arena where it is tested.

The physical expression of integrity — work that tells the truth about who made it. At Stretching Branches, this connection is indirect but foundational: the child who learns to complete tasks fully and honestly is building the disposition that will later produce honest craftsmanship.

content.why_this_matters:
There is something inside every person that knows when they are lying. Even very young children feel it — the tightness in the stomach, the look away, the sudden quietness after something breaks. That feeling is not a punishment. It is a signal. It is telling you that what you said and what actually happened do not match, and that the gap between them is a problem.

This packet is about closing that gap. It is about learning — early, before the stakes get high — to tell the truth even when the truth is uncomfortable. To say "I did it" when you did it, "I broke it" when you broke it, and "I'm sorry" when you are sorry — and then to do something about it.

Most adults struggle with this. Not because they are bad people, but because they never learned, when they were young, that the short-term discomfort of telling the truth is always smaller than the long-term damage of hiding it. The lie that covers a mistake does not make the mistake disappear. It adds a second problem on top of the first one. Now you have the broken thing and the lie about the broken thing, and the lie is almost always worse.

You are going to learn something that will serve you for the rest of your life: **the habit of honesty.** Not perfect honesty — no one is perfectly honest every moment of every day. But the pattern of honesty. The reflex that, when something goes wrong, your first instinct is to say what happened rather than to hide what happened. That reflex, built now, becomes the foundation of something called integrity — which means that the person you are on the inside matches the person you show on the outside. And integrity is the thing that makes people trust you. Without it, nothing else you build will hold.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Truth-telling as reflex** — the habit of stating what actually happened, including and especially when the truth creates short-term discomfort
2. **Owning mistakes directly** — saying "I did it" without deflecting, minimizing, blaming others, or waiting to be caught
3. **Apologizing properly** — specific, sincere, and followed by changed behavior; not the reflexive "sorry" that changes nothing
4. **Restitution** — fixing what you broke, replacing what you lost, repairing what you damaged; action that follows words
5. **Honesty under pressure** — the beginning of the capacity to tell the truth when lying would be easier and carry no visible consequence
6. **Understanding that trust is built by consistent honesty** — the earliest awareness that people believe you because of your pattern, not because of any single statement
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Is the Truth, and Why Does It Matter?

The truth is what actually happened. Not what you wish had happened. Not what would make the other person less upset. Not the version that makes you look better. What actually happened.

This sounds simple. It is not. Even adults struggle with it — not because they do not know what happened, but because telling the truth sometimes costs something. The truth might mean you get in trouble. The truth might mean someone is disappointed in you. The truth might mean you have to fix something, give something back, or admit that you were wrong. And nobody — not children, not adults — enjoys those things.

But here is what you need to understand, and what you will prove to yourself over and over as you grow: **the cost of telling the truth is almost always smaller than the cost of hiding it.**

When you lie about something, the original problem does not go away. The broken plate is still broken. The unfinished chore is still unfinished. The thing you took is still gone. The lie does not fix any of that. What the lie does is add a new problem: now the people around you do not know what actually happened, which means they cannot help you fix it, and — worse — they are making decisions based on something that is not real. And when they eventually find out (and they almost always do), you now have two problems: the original thing, and the fact that you lied about it. The second problem is usually bigger than the first.

There is another reason the truth matters, and it is more important than avoiding trouble: **the truth is what makes trust possible.** When you tell the truth — consistently, over time, even when it costs you — the people around you learn that your words match reality. They learn that when you say something happened, it happened. When you say you did something, you did it. When you say you did not, you did not. This is called being trustworthy, and it is one of the most valuable things a person can be. It takes a long time to build and a very short time to destroy.

Every time you tell the truth, you are adding one small piece to a structure called your reputation. Every time you lie, you are removing a piece. The structure builds slowly and falls quickly. You are building it now, even at your age, even in the small things that do not seem to matter. They all matter.

### Part 2: Owning Your Mistakes

Everyone makes mistakes. You will break things. You will forget things. You will do things you were told not to do. You will hurt someone's feelings. You will make bad choices. This is not a prediction of failure — it is a description of being human. The question is never whether you will make mistakes. The question is what you do after you make them.

There are two paths after a mistake:

**Path one: Own it.** Say what happened. Say what you did. Do not add "but" — "I broke it, but it was an accident" is already half a deflection. Say "I broke it." Full stop. Then: "What do I need to do to fix it?"

**Path two: Hide it.** Say nothing and hope no one notices. Or say "I didn't do it." Or say "it was already like that." Or say "[sibling's name] did it." Or cry so that the attention shifts from what you did to how you feel about what you did — which is a sophisticated form of hiding that even adults use.

Path one is harder in the moment. Path two is harder in every moment after.

When you own a mistake, several things happen:
- The problem gets addressed immediately, while it is still small
- The adults around you can help you fix it
- You demonstrate that you can be trusted to tell the truth even when it is uncomfortable
- You practice a skill — accountability — that becomes stronger each time you use it
- The incident ends. It is dealt with. You move on.

When you hide a mistake, several things happen:
- The problem may get worse while it is hidden
- You carry the weight of knowing something that no one else knows, which is its own form of stress
- If (when) the truth comes out, the original problem now has the additional problem of the cover-up
- The adults around you learn that your first instinct is to hide rather than to own — which damages trust
- The incident does not end. It lingers, waiting to surface.

**Owning a mistake is not the same as enjoying the consequences.** You are not expected to be happy about being in trouble. You are expected to be honest about what happened and willing to make it right. Those are different things. You can dislike the consequence and still accept it with dignity. That is, in fact, exactly what integrity looks like in practice.

### Part 3: The Anatomy of a Real Apology

"Sorry" is one of the most overused and least meaningful words in most households. Children learn to say it reflexively — a quick "sorry" tossed out to end the uncomfortable moment, with no actual change in behavior. This is not an apology. It is a social escape hatch.

A real apology has specific parts, and each part matters:

**1. Name what you did.** Not "sorry for what happened" — that is passive and vague. "I'm sorry I broke your toy." "I'm sorry I hit you." "I'm sorry I lied about eating the cookies." The specific naming matters because it demonstrates that you know exactly what you did wrong. It is not a blanket cover for unspecified wrongs. It is an acknowledgment of a specific action.

**2. Acknowledge the effect.** "I know that made you sad." "I know you were counting on me and I let you down." "I know that was your favorite thing and now it is broken." This part shows that you understand your action had an impact on someone else — that the world does not begin and end with your own experience.

**3. State what you will do differently.** "I will be more careful with other people's things." "I will not hit when I am angry — I will use my words or walk away." "I will tell the truth next time, even if I am scared of getting in trouble." This is the commitment to change. Without it, the apology is just a description of the past with no connection to the future.

**4. Make restitution where possible.** "Can I help fix it?" "I will do your chore today since I broke your thing." "I saved my money and I want to replace what I lost." Restitution is the action that proves the apology is real. Words are necessary but not sufficient. The behavior that follows the words is what demonstrates sincerity.

**What a real apology does NOT include:**
- "I'm sorry, but..." — anything after "but" erases the apology
- "I'm sorry you feel that way" — this apologizes for the other person's reaction, not for your action
- "I said I was sorry!" (when the behavior continues unchanged) — a repeated apology without changed behavior is not an apology; it is a pattern
- Crying as a substitute for words — tears may be genuine, but they are not an apology; the words still need to be said

You will practice this. Many times. And it will feel awkward and uncomfortable at first. That is normal. The discomfort is not a sign that something is wrong — it is the feeling of doing something hard that matters.

### Part 4: Restitution — Making Things Right

Saying sorry is the beginning. Making things right is the completion.

Restitution means doing something to repair the damage your action caused. It is not punishment — it is responsibility. The distinction matters. Punishment is imposed from outside and is often unrelated to the offense. Restitution is directly connected to what happened and is about making the situation as right as it can be made.

**What restitution looks like at your age:**

- You broke someone's toy. You help fix it, or you use your own money (or allowance, or time) to replace it.
- You made a mess that was not yours to make. You clean it up — fully, not partway.
- You said something mean. You apologize specifically, and you do something kind for that person — not as performance, but as genuine repair.
- You did not do your chore and someone else had to do it. You do their chore next time, willingly and fully.
- You lied about something. You go back, tell the truth, and accept whatever consequence comes.

The key principle: **your actions created a cost for someone else. Restitution is you bearing that cost instead of leaving it on them.** This is one of the earliest forms of justice a child can practice. It is not theoretical. It is concrete, immediate, and real.

Some things cannot be fully restored. A broken heirloom cannot be unbroken. Words that hurt someone cannot be unsaid. In these cases, restitution is not about making things exactly as they were — it is about demonstrating, through action, that you understand the weight of what happened and that you are willing to bear a cost to address it. The willingness itself matters.

### Part 5: No Lying to Escape Discomfort

This is the heart of IC1.3 at Stretching Branches, and it is the principle that every other part of this packet supports.

Lying is almost always an attempt to avoid discomfort. The child lies about breaking the vase because they do not want to be scolded. The child lies about finishing their schoolwork because they want to go play. The child lies about hitting their sibling because they do not want the consequence. In every case, the lie is an escape route — a way to get from the uncomfortable present to a more comfortable immediate future.

The problem is that the escape route is a trap. It works in the short term — sometimes. But it trains a reflex that will destroy your life if it becomes your default. The adult who lies to avoid discomfort at work gets fired. The adult who lies to their spouse destroys their marriage. The adult who lies about their finances goes bankrupt. The adult who lies to avoid accountability in leadership harms everyone who depends on them. Every one of those adults started as a child who learned that lying was an effective way to avoid short-term discomfort.

**You are learning the opposite lesson now.** You are learning that discomfort is survivable, that the truth is safer than the lie even when it does not feel that way, and that the person who tells the truth — even when it hurts — is the person others will trust, rely on, and follow.

This does not mean that telling the truth should produce catastrophic consequences. Your mentor has a responsibility to make truth-telling safe — not free of consequences, but safe. If you tell the truth and the response is calm, proportionate correction, you learn: truth-telling leads to manageable outcomes. If you tell the truth and the response is explosive rage, you learn: truth-telling is dangerous. The second lesson produces liars. The first lesson produces people of integrity.

Your part is to tell the truth. Your mentor's part is to receive it in a way that makes the next truth easier to tell. Together, this builds something that neither of you can build alone.

### Part 6: Trust — The Thing You Are Building Without Knowing It

You may not think about trust yet. It is an abstract word for something that operates invisibly. But trust is already being built — or damaged — by everything you do.

Trust is what happens when someone believes you without needing proof. When your parent asks "did you brush your teeth?" and believes your answer — that is trust. When your friend leaves their favorite toy near you and does not worry — that is trust. When your mentor assigns you a task and walks away, confident it will be done — that is trust.

Trust is built by consistent honesty over time. Not one dramatic moment of truth-telling, but hundreds of small moments where you told the truth, did what you said you would do, and owned what you did wrong. Each moment is tiny. The cumulative effect is enormous.

Trust is damaged by dishonesty — and the damage is disproportionate. One lie can undo weeks of honest behavior. This is not fair, but it is real. The reason is simple: once someone knows you are capable of lying to them, they cannot be sure when you are telling the truth. Every statement becomes uncertain. The foundation cracks.

You are building your foundation now. Every truth you tell is a brick. Every lie is a crack. The structure you build in these years — your reputation as a person who can or cannot be trusted — will follow you into every friendship, every team, every job, and every relationship you ever have.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Truth-Telling in the Moment [Daily, As Situations Arise]
When something goes wrong — you break something, forget something, do something you should not have — tell the truth about it immediately. Do not wait to be asked. Do not wait to be caught. Go to your parent or mentor and say what happened. This is practiced until it becomes your first instinct, not your last resort.

### Practice 2: The Full Apology [As Needed]
When you have wronged someone, practice the full apology: name what you did, acknowledge the effect, state what you will do differently, and make restitution. Your mentor will help you through this at first. Over time, you should be able to do it on your own, without coaching.

### Practice 3: Restitution Practice [As Needed]
When your action creates a cost for someone else, identify what you can do to make it right — and do it. This is not negotiated. It is not optional. It is the completion of accountability. If you are unsure what restitution looks like in a specific case, ask your mentor: "What can I do to make this right?"

### Practice 4: The Honesty Check-In [Weekly, With Mentor]
Once a week, as part of your weekly review, your mentor will ask: "Was there a time this week when you were tempted to lie or hide something? What did you do?" This is not an interrogation. It is a conversation. The goal is for you to develop the self-awareness to notice when you are tempted to be dishonest — and to recognize that the temptation itself is normal, and that the choice to be honest despite it is the thing that matters.

### Practice 5: Stories of Integrity [Weekly]
At least once a week, read or hear a story about someone who told the truth at personal cost, or someone whose dishonesty led to real consequences. After the story, discuss: What did they choose? Why was it hard? What happened because of their choice? What would you have done?

### Practice 6: Catching Yourself [Ongoing]
This is the most advanced practice at this tier. When you catch yourself in a lie — even a small one, even one that seems harmless — go back and correct it. "Actually, that is not what happened. Here is what really happened." This is extraordinarily difficult and extraordinarily important. The ability to self-correct after a lie, voluntarily, is a sign of developing integrity that most adults never achieve.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Making Truth-Telling Safe (Not Consequence-Free)

The single most important thing you can do for IC1.3 formation is to separate the consequence for the mistake from the consequence for the lie.

When a child breaks a vase and tells you, the consequence is about the vase: cleanup, restitution, perhaps a brief restriction on roughhousing indoors. When a child breaks a vase and lies about it, the consequence is about both: the vase (same as above) and the lie (additional, and clearly identified as additional).

This separation must be explicit and consistent. The child needs to learn, through repeated experience, that: **"If I tell the truth, I deal with the consequence of what I did. If I lie, I deal with the consequence of what I did PLUS the consequence of lying. Truth-telling is always the better deal."**

This only works if the truth-telling consequence is proportionate and calm. If the consequence for breaking a vase and telling the truth is thirty minutes of angry yelling, the child's cost-benefit analysis will favor lying. You may not intend this, but the child's nervous system does not care about your intentions. It cares about what actually happens when they tell the truth.

**The correction formula for IC1.3 moments:**
1. Receive the confession calmly. Visibly. The child is watching your face and body language before you say a single word.
2. Acknowledge the honesty: "Thank you for telling me the truth." (Brief. Not effusive. Not a reward — an acknowledgment.)
3. Address the action: "The vase is broken. Here is what needs to happen now."
4. Determine restitution: "You need to help clean this up, and we will talk about how to replace it."
5. Follow through. End the incident. Do not return to it repeatedly. Do not bring it up days later as evidence of a pattern. It is done.

If the child lied first and you discovered the truth later:
1. Address the lie directly: "I found out what actually happened with the vase. You told me you did not do it. That was not true."
2. Separate the problems: "There are two things here. The vase, and the lie. Let us deal with both."
3. The vase consequence is the same as it would have been with honesty.
4. The lie consequence is additional and clearly labeled: "Because you chose to hide what happened instead of telling me, there is an additional consequence: [specific, proportionate]."
5. Restate the principle: "If you had told me the truth when it happened, we would only have needed to deal with the vase. The lie made it worse. Next time, tell me the truth. I will be calm, and we will deal with it together."

### Modeling Integrity

Your child is studying you. Not consciously — they are not taking notes. But they are absorbing your behavior as the template for how adults handle truth, mistakes, and accountability. Consider honestly:

- When you make a mistake, do you own it? Aloud? In front of the child? Or do you explain it away, blame circumstances, or pretend it did not happen?
- When you are wrong in an argument with your spouse, do you say so? Or do you defend your position past the point where you know you are wrong?
- When you forget a commitment you made to the child, do you acknowledge it and apologize? Or do you rewrite the history? ("I never said that" when you did.)
- When you lose your temper disproportionately, do you come back and say, "I handled that badly. I am sorry. Here is what I should have done"?

Every one of these moments is IC1.3 formation — yours and the child's, simultaneously. The adult who models accountability normalizes accountability. The adult who models defensiveness normalizes defensiveness.

This is not about performing perfection. It is about demonstrating what integrity looks like in practice: imperfect, sometimes late, but real. The parent who says "I should not have yelled. That was my mistake, not yours. I am sorry, and I will work on it" is teaching IC1.3 more effectively than any curriculum could.

### The Environment of Proportionate Consequences

Natural consequences are the primary teacher at this age. The child who lies about finishing their schoolwork discovers that the work is still there tomorrow — plus the trust issue. The child who breaks something and hides it loses access to the context where the breaking happened. The child who hits a sibling and denies it finds that the sibling is less willing to play with them.

The key: consequences should be proportionate, natural where possible, and completed. A consequence that goes on indefinitely — weeks of being reminded of a single lie — teaches the child that confession leads to unending punishment, which is a powerful incentive to lie better, not to lie less. When restitution is made and the consequence is served, the matter is closed. Genuinely closed. Not "closed but I will mention it next time you do something wrong."
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Voluntary Truth-Telling

**Developing well:** The child tells you about mistakes before you discover them. They come to you and say what happened. This may be tearful — that is fine. The tears are about the discomfort of the situation, and the child is choosing honesty despite the discomfort. The content matters more than the composure.

**Developing slowly:** The child only admits truth when confronted with evidence. Left to their own devices, they default to concealment. Truth comes out under questioning, not voluntarily.

**Diagnostic questions:** How do you respond when the child confesses? Is your initial reaction calm, or does the child see anger before they see acknowledgment? Have there been incidents where confession led to disproportionate consequences? If so, the child has been trained to conceal, and the training needs to be reversed — by you, not by the child.

### 2. Owning Mistakes Without Deflection

**Developing well:** "I did it." No qualifiers. No blaming the sibling, the dog, the weather, or the unfairness of the universe. The child takes ownership of their action directly.

**Developing slowly:** Every admission comes with a deflection: "I did it, but she started it." "I broke it, but it was already wobbly." "I lied, but you would have been mad." The child is technically admitting the action but distributing the responsibility away from themselves.

**Diagnostic questions:** Do the adults in the household model unqualified ownership? Or do you say things like "I was late, but traffic was terrible"? Children learn deflection from the deflectors around them. Also consider: is the child deflecting because the unqualified admission historically produces an unmanageable response? If "I did it" leads to the same calm correction regardless of preamble, the deflection will fade.

### 3. Proper Apology

**Developing well:** The child can, with coaching initially and increasingly on their own, deliver a specific apology: name the action, acknowledge the impact, state what they will do differently. The apology feels genuine — not robotic, not performative.

**Developing slowly:** "Sorry." One word. Reflexive. No specificity. No behavioral commitment. The child has learned that the word "sorry" ends uncomfortable moments, not that apology is a meaningful act of repair.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you taught the anatomy of a real apology? Many children say "sorry" reflexively because that is all they have been taught. Walk them through it: "What are you sorry for?" "How did that affect [person]?" "What will you do differently?" The first several times will feel scripted. Over time, it becomes internalized.

### 4. Restitution

**Developing well:** The child identifies (or accepts when directed) what they can do to make the situation right, and does it. They understand the connection between their action and the repair.

**Developing slowly:** The child resists restitution — "it is not fair," "why do I have to?" — or performs it minimally and resentfully. The connection between action and repair is not yet internalized.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the restitution proportionate and connected to the action? A child who broke a toy and is told to clean their entire room is not experiencing restitution — they are experiencing punishment labeled as restitution. True restitution is directly linked: you broke it, you help fix or replace it. Also: are you following through? Stated restitution that is not enforced teaches the child that restitution is optional.

### 5. No Lying to Escape Discomfort

**Developing well:** The child's default is shifting from concealment to disclosure. When something goes wrong, their first instinct — not every time, but increasingly — is to report rather than hide. They tolerate the discomfort of potential consequences without being paralyzed by it.

**Developing slowly:** The child consistently lies when the truth would produce consequences. The lying may be unsophisticated (denying something obvious) or increasingly sophisticated (constructing plausible alternative narratives). Either way, deception remains the default response to discomfort.

**Critical intervention point:** If the child is lying consistently and the lying is not improving, the problem is almost certainly environmental, not characterological. Ask: What happens when this child tells the truth? If the honest answer is "they get yelled at," "they get a disproportionate punishment," or "they get a lecture that lasts twenty minutes," the child is behaving rationally by lying. Fix the environment. Make truth-telling produce a better outcome than lying. The behavior will follow.

### 6. Self-Correction After Lies

**Developing well:** The child catches themselves in a lie and comes back to correct it. "I said I did not eat the cookies. That was not true. I ate them." This is a very advanced marker at this age. When it appears, it is a strong signal of genuine integrity formation.

**Developing slowly:** The child does not self-correct. Lies stand unless externally discovered. This is normal for the younger end of Stretching Branches and is not cause for alarm — it is the goal to work toward, not the starting expectation.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Creating a Culture of Fear Around Mistakes
The most common IC1.3 failure is an environment where the consequence for mistakes is so severe — emotionally if not physically — that the child learns hiding is safer than honesty. If you find yourself consistently discovering lies rather than receiving confessions, look at your own response pattern first. The child is not defective. The incentive structure is.

### Punishing the Confession Instead of the Action
When a child confesses and the response is "HOW COULD YOU DO THAT?" the child receives punishment for confessing. The action produced the consequence, but the confession is what triggered the adult's emotional response. The child's takeaway: confessing causes pain. This is exactly backwards from what IC1.3 requires.

### The Interrogation Trap
Some parents, upon suspecting a lie, interrogate the child: "Are you SURE? Think about it. Are you REALLY sure?" This teaches the child that lies must be sustained under pressure — a skill you do not want to develop. If you know the truth, state it: "I know what happened. I want to give you a chance to tell me, but I already know." If you do not know the truth, ask once, accept the answer, and address any discrepancy when evidence appears. Do not train your child to become a better liar under interrogation.

### Over-Praising Honesty
When a child tells the truth and receives effusive praise — "I am SO PROUD of you for telling the truth! That was SO BRAVE!" — the child learns that honesty is an exceptional act worthy of celebration. It should not be. Honesty should be the baseline, unremarkable and expected. A brief acknowledgment ("Thank you for telling me") is sufficient. Save the celebration for moments that genuinely exceed expectations.

### Making Restitution Punitive
Restitution is repair, not punishment. A child who broke a plate and is made to hand-wash every dish in the house for a week is not learning restitution — they are learning that admitting breakage leads to disproportionate labor. Restitution should be proportionate and directly connected: you broke the plate, you help clean it up, you contribute to replacing it in a way that is appropriate to your age and capacity.

### The Repeat Apology Problem
If the child is apologizing for the same behavior repeatedly with no change, the apology system has become performative. "Sorry" has become a tax they pay to continue the behavior. When this happens, shift from apology to consequence: "We are past apologies for this. You know what is expected. The consequence is [specific]. When the behavior changes, that is the real apology."
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What Does It Mean to Tell the Truth?
**Strong:** The child can articulate that telling the truth means saying what actually happened, even when it is uncomfortable. They give a specific example of a time they told a hard truth. They demonstrate understanding that truth-telling is difficult but important.
**Weak:** "It means not lying." No depth, no example, no understanding of why it matters.

### Prompt 2: What Happens When Someone Lies?
**Strong:** The child describes consequences beyond punishment — trust damage, the lie making things worse, the feeling of carrying a secret. They understand that lying affects the relationship, not just the immediate situation.
**Weak:** "You get in trouble." The child sees lying only through the lens of detection and punishment, not through the lens of trust and relationship.

### Prompt 3: A Time You Owned a Mistake
**Strong:** Specific incident. Clear description of what they did, what they said, how they felt (uncomfortable but relieved), and what happened after (consequence was manageable, the incident ended).
**Weak:** Cannot recall an example, or the example involves being caught rather than voluntarily confessing.

### Prompt 4: What Is a Real Apology?
**Strong:** Can name the parts: say what you did, say how it affected the other person, say what you will do differently, make it right. Demonstrates understanding that "sorry" alone is not sufficient.
**Weak:** "You say sorry." No understanding of the components or the purpose of genuine apology.

### Prompt 5: Someone From a Story Who Was Honest
**Strong:** Names a specific person and a specific situation. Can articulate what honesty cost them and why they chose it anyway. Shows engagement with the moral weight of the story.
**Weak:** Generic response. "A person was honest." No specifics, no understanding of the cost.

### Prompt 6: Making Something Right
**Strong:** Describes a specific instance of restitution — what they broke or damaged, what they did to repair it. Understands the connection between action and repair. Shows willingness, not just compliance.
**Weak:** "I said sorry." Restitution is equated with apology, with no understanding of the action component.
